jokes.text: Ever since the recession hit, waves of new people are suddenly broke. These people have no idea how to live without money. They're what's called 'new poor.' We're old poor.
jokes.setup: Discussion about someone living in an RV on their street
jokes.punchline: The distinction between 'new poor' and 'old poor' as if poverty has social classes
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-driven comedy that establishes the gang's delusional worldview. The idea of poverty having a class system is both absurd and perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Ever since the recession hit, waves of new people are suddenly broke. These people have no idea how to live without money. They're what's called 'new poor.' We're old poor.

jokes.text: Have some class if you're gonna be poor!
jokes.setup: Lecture about how 'old poor' people like them have dignity
jokes.punchline: The oxymoronic concept of having 'class' while being poor
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character voice, building on the 'old poor vs new poor' concept with aristocratic delusion.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Have some class if you're gonna be poor!

jokes.text: Well, you start putting plans under microscopes, nothing's gonna make sense, all right?
jokes.setup: Mac points out that slashing tires won't make someone leave
jokes.punchline: Charlie's defense that scrutinizing any plan makes it fall apart
jokes.explanation: Classic Charlie logic that's both stupid and oddly philosophical. Became a quotable line about avoiding critical thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Well, you start putting plans under microscopes, nothing's gonna make sense, all right?

jokes.text: Dee! Sharpest item in the bar! I need it now! Let's go!
jokes.setup: Frank dramatically calling for something sharp
jokes.punchline: The reveal that he's trying to kill himself over losing money
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor setup that escalates quickly from mundane bar request to suicide attempt.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Dee! Sharpest item in the bar! I need it now! Let's go!

jokes.text: His neck is so thick, I feel like he's just gonna swing and dangle around for a really long time.
jokes.setup: Frank attempting suicide by hanging
jokes.punchline: Casual observation about the physics of his failed suicide attempt
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark and casual approach to suicide. The clinical observation mixed with the absurd visual creates perfect dark comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: His neck is so thick, I feel like he's just gonna swing and dangle around for a really long time.

jokes.text: Frank, it's not gonna work for you. Your neck's too thick, buddy.
jokes.setup: Frank's failed suicide attempt
jokes.punchline: Helpful advice about why his suicide method won't work
jokes.explanation: Charlie's helpful, matter-of-fact tone about suicide mechanics is perfectly inappropriate and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Frank, it's not gonna work for you. Your neck's too thick, buddy.

jokes.text: Maybe we should take this time to streamline Paddy's, make it run as efficiently as the government.
jokes.setup: Discussion about making the bar more efficient during tough times
jokes.punchline: Using the notoriously inefficient government as a model for efficiency
jokes.explanation: Solid but predictable government inefficiency joke. Works but isn't particularly innovative.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Maybe we should take this time to streamline Paddy's, make it run as efficiently as the government.

jokes.text: Frank, don't be a moron. You start cutting bonuses, you're gonna lose your top guys.
jokes.setup: Discussion about cutting costs at the failing bar
jokes.punchline: Calling themselves 'top guys' while running a dive bar into the ground
jokes.explanation: Good character delusion about their value as employees. Dennis's narcissism shines through.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Frank, don't be a moron. You start cutting bonuses, you're gonna lose your top guys.

jokes.text: No, we're chaff. No, we're not. We're- We're wheat. Why would you want to be wheat? Why would you ever want to be chaff?
jokes.setup: Frank says they need to separate wheat from chaff
jokes.punchline: Neither understanding the metaphor and arguing about which they want to be
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalating confusion about a basic metaphor, showcasing their fundamental stupidity.

dialogue.line: No, we're chaff.
No, we're not.
We're- We're wheat.
Why would you want to be wheat?
Why would you ever want to be chaff?

jokes.text: We have no business sense? He's the one dangling from a noose 10 minutes ago.
jokes.setup: Frank criticizing their business sense
jokes.punchline: Pointing out Frank's recent suicide attempt as evidence of poor judgment
jokes.explanation: Good callback that uses Frank's earlier breakdown to deflect criticism. Solid structure and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: We have no business sense? He's the one dangling from a noose 10 minutes ago.

jokes.text: Dee, we don't know what it is that you do around here. You don't seem to do anything.
jokes.setup: Asking Dee to defend her job
jokes.punchline: Blunt statement that she appears to contribute nothing
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment showing the group's casual cruelty to Dee. Typical workplace harassment comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Dee, we don't know what it is that you do around here. You don't seem to do anything.

jokes.text: But the mountains, they've turned blue.
jokes.setup: Asked to make a mixed drink instead of just opening a beer
jokes.punchline: Desperate appeal to the color-changing marketing gimmick on beer bottles
jokes.explanation: Great absurd logic showing Dee's incompetence. The desperate appeal to marketing as a reason not to work is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: But the mountains, they've turned blue.

jokes.text: I quit.
jokes.setup: Being pressured to make a mojito to prove her worth
jokes.punchline: Immediately giving up rather than attempting basic bartending
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character moment. Two words that completely capture Dee's incompetence and pride.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: I quit.

jokes.text: I am taking it to the furnace... This bar runs on trash.
jokes.setup: Asked about proper trash disposal
jokes.punchline: Revelation that he's been burning trash to heat the bar
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Charlie logic and absurd environmental solution. The casual delivery of this insane revelation is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I am taking it to the furnace... This bar runs on trash.

jokes.text: Well, I could put the trash into a landfill where it's gonna stay for millions of years, or I could burn it up and get a nice, smoky smell in here and let that smoke go into the sky where it turns into stars.
jokes.setup: Mac saying burning trash isn't green
jokes.punchline: Charlie's completely wrong but confident explanation of how smoke becomes stars
jokes.explanation: One of the series' best Charlie moments. The pseudo-scientific confidence combined with completely wrong information is brilliant. Became a legendary quote.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Well, I could put the trash into a landfill where it's gonna stay for millions of years, or I could burn it up and get a nice, smoky smell in here and let that smoke go into the sky where it turns into stars.

jokes.text: That doesn't sound right, but I don't know enough about stars to dispute it.
jokes.setup: Charlie's insane explanation about smoke turning into stars
jokes.punchline: Mac's admission that he can't argue with obvious nonsense
jokes.explanation: Perfect straight-man response that became equally quotable. The admission of ignorance as a way to avoid confronting obvious lies is brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: That doesn't sound right, but I don't know enough about stars to dispute it.

jokes.text: Charlie, that sign does not say 'Coors.'... It says 'closed,' Charlie!
jokes.setup: Charlie explaining his daily routine of turning on the 'Coors' sign
jokes.punchline: The sign actually says 'CLOSED' and he's been illiterate this whole time
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reveal about Charlie's illiteracy disguised as a simple misunderstanding. Shows how his worldview is completely detached from reality.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Charlie, that sign does not say 'Coors.'... It says 'closed,' Charlie!

jokes.text: So you're gonna throw all the trash in the furnace yourselves. And you're gonna filter the rainwater barrel all by yourselves... and siphon it from all around the neighborhood.
jokes.setup: Being fired from his various 'duties' at the bar
jokes.punchline: List of increasingly illegal and insane things he's been doing
jokes.explanation: Good escalation revealing the full scope of Charlie's insane operations. Each revelation gets worse.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: So you're gonna throw all the trash in the furnace yourselves. And you're gonna filter the rainwater barrel all by yourselves... and siphon it from all around the neighborhood.

jokes.text: We're gonna call a trash man, an electrician, and a plumber and not turn the closed sign on when the bar is open!
jokes.setup: Charlie listing his illegal contributions to the bar
jokes.punchline: The obvious solutions that normal businesses use
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man response highlighting how they've been operating like insane people.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: We're gonna call a trash man, an electrician, and a plumber and not turn the closed sign on when the bar is open!

jokes.text: Honey, I'm home! Feast your eyes on the new headquarters of Reynolds and Reynolds.
jokes.setup: Dee announcing her return with fanfare
jokes.punchline: The 'headquarters' is clearly just an RV in the parking lot
jokes.explanation: Solid visual contrast between grandiose announcement and pathetic reality. Classic Sunny delusion.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Honey, I'm home! Feast your eyes on the new headquarters of Reynolds and Reynolds.

jokes.text: Well, what the hell, dude? I don't know if I can live in a trailer, man. You don't have to, Charlie. It's kinda tight in there already.
jokes.setup: Charlie learning his apartment was sublet
jokes.punchline: Being immediately excluded from the RV as well
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Charlie's homelessness situation with casual cruelty from his friends.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Well, what the hell, dude? I don't know if I can live in a trailer, man.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: You don't have to, Charlie. It's kinda tight in there already.

jokes.text: We're not in the business of giving out handouts, Charlie. We're not the government.
jokes.setup: Charlie asking to live with them
jokes.punchline: Rejecting charity while literally living off Frank's money in an RV
jokes.explanation: Perfect hypocrisy showing Dennis's complete lack of self-awareness while being dependent on others.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: We're not in the business of giving out handouts, Charlie. We're not the government.

jokes.text: That's a $200 shoe! Look at that. Feast your eyes on your new business. Knives.
jokes.setup: Frank asking for Dee's shoe for a demonstration
jokes.punchline: Destroying expensive property to demonstrate knife sales
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy setup showing Frank's destructive approach to business.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: That's a $200 shoe!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Look at that. Feast your eyes on your new business. Knives.

jokes.text: You want me to sell knives door-to-door? No. You're selling vacuums.
jokes.setup: Dee's reasonable assumption about the knife demonstration
jokes.punchline: Frank's nonsensical division of labor in their scam
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing the arbitrary nature of their business plan.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: You want me to sell knives door-to-door?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: No. You're selling vacuums.

jokes.text: [Motor Roaring] Oh! [Shouts] [Motor Continues Roaring]
jokes.setup: Frank demonstrating the wet vac's power
jokes.punchline: The vacuum is so loud and powerful it's completely impractical
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy bit. The extended vacuum noise is more endurance test than clever writing.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Oh! [Shouts]

jokes.text: Mine does not, and believe me, I've tried at several locations... There's one in Franklin Mills I haven't tried. I feel like that could maybe work.
jokes.setup: Dennis explaining Dave & Buster's cards don't work elsewhere
jokes.punchline: Mac's continued belief that one specific location might work
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-specific stupidity. Mac's inability to grasp basic concepts despite repeated experience.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Mine does not, and believe me, I've tried at several locations...

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: There's one in Franklin Mills I haven't tried. I feel like that could maybe work.

jokes.text: It doesn't work anywhere else. It's only gonna work here... Then afterwards, we'll go out to Fox Chase and use the Power Card at that Friday's out there.
jokes.setup: Dennis getting frustrated explaining the cards don't work elsewhere
jokes.punchline: Mac immediately suggesting another location to try
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of Mac's obliviousness. The immediate return to the same mistake shows his complete inability to learn.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: It doesn't work anywhere else. It's only gonna work here...

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Then afterwards, we'll go out to Fox Chase and use the Power Card at that Friday's out there.

jokes.text: What are you talking about? You don't make house payments. You own this house. You've owned it for years. Mom, that's crazy. You collect Social Security.
jokes.setup: Charlie's mom claiming she needs money for house payments
jokes.punchline: Charlie pointing out the obvious lies while being rejected
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing family dysfunction. Charlie being the voice of reason while being abandoned is darkly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? You don't make house payments. You own this house. You've owned it for years. Mom, that's crazy. You collect Social Security.

jokes.text: You know, you and me palling around, getting nuts, doing crazy, fun things... It's stuff that relatives do.
jokes.setup: Uncle Jack suggesting Charlie share his room
jokes.punchline: Vaguely inappropriate suggestions disguised as normal family activities
jokes.explanation: Good uncomfortable comedy hinting at inappropriate behavior while maintaining plausible deniability.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Jack
dialogue.line: You know, you and me palling around, getting nuts, doing crazy, fun things... It's stuff that relatives do.

jokes.text: You ever seen wrestling on television? I am well aware of what wrestling is. It's just not what uncles do to their nephews.
jokes.setup: Uncle Jack's wrestling reference
jokes.punchline: Charlie's awareness that this isn't normal uncle behavior
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy with perfect economy. Charlie's matter-of-fact recognition of inappropriate behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Uncle Jack
dialogue.line: You ever seen wrestling on television?

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I am well aware of what wrestling is. It's just not what uncles do to their nephews.

jokes.text: Actually, I'm vegan. Okay. Then pretend this shoe is whatever you people eat. Maybe it is a shoe.
jokes.setup: Frank's chicken demonstration failing because the customer is vegan
jokes.punchline: Frank's casual dismissiveness and suggestion vegans might eat shoes
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Frank's complete inability to adapt his sales pitch. The casual ignorance is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Beth
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm vegan.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Okay. Then pretend this shoe is whatever you people eat. Maybe it is a shoe.

jokes.text: Owww! Ohh! Is this part of the whole thing? Oh, I cut myself! Ohh! It's a deep cut! I see your bones!
jokes.setup: Frank demonstrating the knife's sharpness
jokes.punchline: Badly injuring himself and the customer's horrified reaction
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy escalation. Works but isn't particularly sophisticated.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Owww! Ohh! Is this part of the whole thing? Oh, I cut myself! Ohh! It's a deep cut! I see your bones!

jokes.text: Oh, shoot! Oh! Oh, I'm missing it! Hey! Oh, no! You're doing that on purpose!
jokes.setup: Dee trying to demonstrate the wet vac on wine stains
jokes.punchline: Completely failing and the customer realizing it's intentional
jokes.explanation: Weak physical comedy that relies on competence failure without much wit or character insight.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Oh, shoot! Oh! Oh, I'm missing it! Hey! Oh, no!

dialogue.speaker: Beth
dialogue.line: You're doing that on purpose!

jokes.text: See, by transferring all of our cash into these Paddy's Dollars and then printing more of these babies than we ever had in cash, we've upped our value.
jokes.setup: Explaining their economic plan
jokes.punchline: Completely misunderstanding how money and value work
jokes.explanation: Good satire of economic illiteracy with confident delivery of completely wrong information.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: See, by transferring all of our cash into these Paddy's Dollars and then printing more of these babies than we ever had in cash, we've upped our value.

jokes.text: Good-bye, recession, hello, depression. You're very lucky that you're with somebody as resilient as Frank Reynolds.
jokes.setup: Frank pivoting after their business failures
jokes.punchline: Calling himself resilient while declaring economic catastrophe
jokes.explanation: Good character contradiction showing Frank's delusion about his competence despite constant failures.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Good-bye, recession, hello, depression. You're very lucky that you're with somebody as resilient as Frank Reynolds.

jokes.text: You've tried to off yourself two times in the last 24 hours, Frank!
jokes.setup: Frank calling himself resilient
jokes.punchline: Pointing out his recent suicide attempts
jokes.explanation: Good callback that uses established events to deflate Frank's self-aggrandizement.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: You've tried to off yourself two times in the last 24 hours, Frank!

jokes.text: These are crabs! Fresh, local Delaware runoff crabs... Yeah, well- They look like sea scorpions.
jokes.setup: Charlie presenting his new business idea
jokes.punchline: The 'crabs' clearly being something horrifying and inedible
jokes.explanation: Great Charlie moment showing his complete disconnect from reality and food safety.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: These are crabs! Fresh, local Delaware runoff crabs...

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Yeah, well- They look like sea scorpions.

jokes.text: Yeah, there's a pretty bad sewage runoff, but, you know, crabs is sewage-proof!
jokes.setup: Concern about sewage contamination
jokes.punchline: Charlie's confident assertion that crabs are 'sewage-proof'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Charlie logic creating a completely nonsensical but confidently delivered food safety claim.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Yeah, there's a pretty bad sewage runoff, but, you know, crabs is sewage-proof!

jokes.text: We're crab people now. We'll live and die by the crab, Dee! We'll eat off the fat of the sea.
jokes.setup: Frank embracing Charlie's crab business
jokes.punchline: Dramatic declaration about becoming 'crab people'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with quotable delivery. Frank's dramatic commitment to obviously bad plans is character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: We're crab people now. We'll live and die by the crab, Dee! We'll eat off the fat of the sea.

jokes.text: How does this work, Mac? The money keeps moving in a circle. But- But we don't have any money. All we have is this.
jokes.setup: Realizing their economic plan doesn't work
jokes.punchline: The slow recognition that they've created worthless paper
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the inevitable collapse of their delusional plan.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: How does this work, Mac? The money keeps moving in a circle. But- But we don't have any money. All we have is this.

jokes.text: Oh, I blacked out that night. Shit, dude! I've been following your lead!
jokes.setup: Mac asking if Dennis came up with their plan
jokes.punchline: Dennis revealing he was blackout drunk and doesn't remember
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation revealing their entire plan was based on drunk rambling. Perfect character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Oh, I blacked out that night. Shit, dude! I've been following your lead!

jokes.text: I don't understand how the U.S. economy works, much less some sort of a self-sustaining one.
jokes.setup: Trying to figure out how their economy should work
jokes.punchline: Mac's honest admission of complete economic ignorance
jokes.explanation: Brilliant honest moment that became highly quotable. The deadpan delivery of complete ignorance is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: I don't understand how the U.S. economy works, much less some sort of a self-sustaining one.

jokes.text: The government is reimbursing me all the money I lost! I love this country! Baby, I'm back!
jokes.setup: Everyone struggling with their failed schemes
jokes.punchline: Frank's sudden bailout success and patriotic celebration
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and timing. Frank's sudden reversal of fortune provides perfect ironic resolution.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: The government is reimbursing me all the money I lost! I love this country! Baby, I'm back!
